Name: Acute Flaccid Paralysis
Description: Sudden onset of limb weakness; surveillance trigger for poliovirus.
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: This will depend on the etiologic agent.

Name: Acute Severe Hepatitis of Unknown Origin in Children
Description: Severe paediatric hepatitis of undetermined cause.
Communicable Disease::Source Guideline: Acute Severe Hepatitis of Unknown Origin in Children.pdf

Name: Amoebiasis
Description: Intestinal protozoan infection; can cause liver abscess.
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period of amoebiasis ranges from a few days to several months or years but most commonly two to four weeks.(2,5) Extra-intestinal manifestations may take much longer.

Name: Anthrax
Description: Zoonotic bacterial infection; cutaneous, GI, inhalational forms.
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period of anthrax varies based on the route of exposure: - Cutaneous: Typically 5–7 days, but can range from 1–12 days.(1–3) - Gastrointestinal: 1–6 days.(1) - Injection: 1–10 days.(1) - Inhalation: due to spore dormancy in l

Name: Botulism
Description: Intoxication by botulinum neurotoxin causing flaccid paralysis.

Name: Brucellosis
Description: Zoonotic bacterial infection from livestock/unpasteurized dairy.
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period is highly variable; generally 1–2 months, with a range of 5 days to 5 months.(4)

Name: California Serogroup Infection
Description: Mosquito-borne arboviral infection; can cause encephalitis.
Communicable Disease::Pathogen: California serogroup viruses (e.g., Jamestown Canyon, Snowshoe Hare)
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period of CSG viruses is between five and 15 days.(7)

Name: Campylobacteriosis
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: Usually two to five days, but can range from one to 10 days depending on the dose ingested.(2)

Name: Carbapenemase-Producing Organisms (CPOs)
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: Unknown. Symptomatic CPO infection can occur weeks to years after being initially colonized, especially in individuals who are immunocompromised (See Host Susceptibility).(12,13)

Name: Cerebrospinal Fluid Isolates
Description: Surveillance category for organisms isolated from cerebrospinal fluid.

Name: Chancroid
Description: Sexually transmitted bacterial genital ulcer disease.
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period generally lasts three to five days but may extend to 14 days.(8)

Name: Chikungunya Virus (CHIKV)
Description: Mosquito-borne arboviral febrile illness with arthralgia.
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period is typically 3–7 days (range, 1–12 days).(11)

Name: Chlamydia
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period is variable depending upon the type/site of infection. It is commonly 7–14 days, but can be as long as six weeks.

Name: Cholera (O1 and O139)
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period ranges from a few hours to five days.(9,11)

Name: Congenital Rubella Infection Syndrome
Description: Congenital infection causing birth defects; vaccine-preventable.
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: Not applicable.

Name: Congenital Syphilis
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: Incubation period is not clearly defined, as exposures happen in utero. Identification of a timeline from exposure to symptom development is difficult to determine due to the pregnancy.(18)

Name: Coronavirus, COVID-19
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period for SARS-CoV-2 may differ depending on the VOC.

Name: Creutzfeldt-Jakob Disease (CJD)
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period for naturally occurring sCJD is not known.

Name: Cryptosporidiosis
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period is not precisely known. It has been suggested that one to 12 days is the likely range, with an average of seven days.

Name: Cyclosporiasis
Description: Protozoan enteric infection from contaminated produce.
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period averages about one week with a range of one to 11 days.

Name: Cytomegalovirus, Congenital (CMV)
Description: Congenital viral infection; leading non-genetic cause of hearing loss.
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: Incubation period varies with the route of infection (i.e., congenital vs perinatal) and the size of inoculum.(9)

Name: Dengue
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period ranges from 3 – 14 days, but is typically 4 – 7 days.(3)

Name: Diphtheria
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period for respiratory diphtheria is, typically two to five days with a range of one to 10 days.(2,3) The incubation period for cutaneous diphtheria may be longer as it is not well defined.(11)

Name: Disease in Unusual Form
Description: Alert category for atypical presentations of notifiable disease.

Name: Eastern Equine Encephalitis
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period for EEE is usually five to 15 days.

Name: Ebola Virus Disease
Description: Severe viral haemorrhagic fever; contact with bodily fluids.
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: Symptoms take between two to 21 days to appear, but usually present within four to nine days.(6–8)

Name: Echinococcosis (Alveolar)
Description: Zoonotic tapeworm infection forming hepatic cysts.
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period ranges from 12 months to years, depending on the number of larvae ingested, the location of the cysts and how fast they grow.(2)

Name: Enterovirus D68 (EV-D68)
Description: Respiratory enterovirus; linked to acute flaccid myelitis.

Name: Escherichia coli, Shiga Toxin-Producing (STEC) Infections
Description: Toxin-producing enteric infection; can cause HUS.
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period for most STEC serotypes is typically 3 - 4 days with a range of 2 - 10 days.(3) As the incubation period for individual cases can vary, clinical discretion is required when determining where and how the disease was lik

Name: Giardiasis
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period ranges from three to 25 or more days, but is usually between seven to 10 days.(1)

Name: Gonorrhea
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period is typically two to seven days, with a range of 1–14 days.(6)

Name: Haemolytic Uremic Syndrome
Description: Thrombotic microangiopathy; sequela of STEC infection.
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: This will depend on the etiologic agent. Refer to the specific guideline.

Name: Haemophilus Influenzae, Invasive
Description: Invasive bacterial disease; serotype b vaccine-preventable.
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period remains unknown but is probably two to four days. (5)

Name: Hantavirus
Description: Rodent-borne viral disease causing pulmonary syndrome.
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period is approximately two weeks, ranging from a few days to six weeks.

Name: Hepatitis A
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period of HA infection can vary between 15 and 50 days, depending on the infectious dose, with an average of 28 to 30 days.(1)

Name: Hepatitis B - Acute and Chronic
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period for acute infections is 45 to 180 days, with an average of 60 to 90 days. HBsAg may appear in as little as two weeks, but in rare cases, as long as six to nine months.

Name: Hepatitis C (Acute Case)
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period ranges from 14 days to six months, commonly six to nine weeks.(1) After exposure, HCV RNA can be detected in plasma within days, often one to four weeks before liver enzymes rise.(8) There is normally a time lapse of s

Name: Hepatitis C (Chronic Case)
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period ranges from 14 days to six months, commonly six to nine weeks.(1) After exposure, HCV RNA can be detected in plasma within days, often one to four weeks before liver enzymes rise.(8) There is normally a time lapse of s

Name: Hepatitis E
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period of HEV is 26–42 days but can range from 15–64 days.(3)

Name: Histoplasmosis
Description: Environmental fungal infection from inhaled spores.
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: Symptoms usually appear within three to 17 days after exposure; the average is 10 days.(3) This may be shorter if the exposure is greater.

Name: Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV)
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period is variable.

Name: Influenza, Avian (Includes H5N1 and H7N9)
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period for AI A(H7N9) has been reported as 5 to 6 days, with a range from 1 to 15 days.(G)(5) The incubation period for AI A(H5N1) has been reported as 1 to 5 days, and up to 7 days.(15)

Name: Influenza, Seasonal
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period for influenza is generally one to four days with an average of two days.(1)

Name: Japanese Encephalitis
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period is four to 15 days, average of six to eight days.(1)

Name: Legionellosis
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period has a range of two to 14 days and usually averages five to six days.(4,7,8) Some cases may have an extended incubation period of up to 19 days.

Name: Leprosy
Description: Chronic bacterial infection of skin and nerves.
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period is long, ranging from one to several years, but is typically three to five years. Consequently, it is difficult to determine where or when the disease was contracted.

Name: Listeriosis
Description: Foodborne bacterial infection; severe in pregnancy.
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period ranges from three to 70 days, with the median incubation period estimated to be three weeks.

Name: Lyme Disease
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period from infection to the onset of EM is typically seven to 14 days, but may be as short as three days and as long as 30 days.(46,49)

Name: Lymphogranuloma Venereum
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period is variable ranging from three to 30 days for a primary lesion. If buboes are the first identified symptom, the incubation period is two to six weeks.(3)

Name: MPOX
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period of MPOX is usually between 6–13 days but can range from 5–21 days.(8)

Name: Malaria
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: Varies depending on species with approximate ranges as follows:(3) - P. falciparum: 6 to 14 days, - P. ovale: 12 to 18 days, - P. vivax: 12 to 18 days, may extend up to 6 to 12 months in temperate regions, - P. knowlesi: 9 to 12 days, - P.

Name: Measles
Description: Highly contagious airborne viral rash illness; vaccine-preventable.
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: Time from exposure to first signs of disease is approximately 10 days with a range of 7–18 days.(2) The incubation period from exposure to rash onset averages 14 days but the rash can appear as late as 19–21 days from infection.(1,2) Indivi

Name: Meningococcal Disease, Invasive
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period varies from two to 10 days, most commonly three to four days.(8)

Name: Muco-Purulent Cervicitis
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period is usually one to three weeks, depending on the organism involved.

Name: Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in Children (MIS-C)
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: Presentation of MIS-C symptoms following COVID-19 infection range approximately 2-6 weeks following infection.(2,8)

Name: Mumps
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period of mumps is typically 16–18 days, and can range between 12–25 days.(1)

Name: Neonatal Herpes Simplex Infection
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period varies from two to 12 days. In newborns, the infection may be present at birth or may occur as late as four weeks postpartum.

Name: Non-Gonococcal Urethritis
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: One to five weeks (usually two to three weeks) after sexual contact.(6)

Name: Paratyphoid Fever
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period ranges from 1–10 days depending on the size of infecting dose and host factors.(1)

Name: Pertussis
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period is usually seven to 10 days with a range of five to 21 days.(1,3)

Name: Pneumococcal Disease, Invasive
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period is not well determined but is thought to be as short as 1 to 3 days.(2)

Name: Poliomyelitis
Description: Faecal-oral viral infection causing paralysis; vaccine-preventable.
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period is 3–6 days for non-specific symptom onset. For paralytic polio, the incubation period is commonly 7–21 days with a reported range of 3–35 days.(2,4) The incubation period for non-paralytic cases is up to six weeks.

Name: Powassan Encephalitis
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period of POW is usually seven to 14 days.

Name: Psittacosis
Description: Zoonotic bacterial respiratory infection from birds.
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period is between one to four weeks, commonly ten days.

Name: Q Fever
Description: Zoonotic bacterial infection; airborne from livestock.
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period is dependent on the size of the infectious dose but is typically 20 days with a range of 14–39 days. Acute Q fever can develop years after an initial infection.

Name: Rabies
Description: Zoonotic viral encephalitis from animal bites; preventable post-exposure.
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period varies from a few days to more than 19 years; however, the majority of cases become ill within 90 days of exposure.(2,3,8,16) The incubation period is dependent on a number of factors, including the severity of the wou

Name: Rare or Emerging Communicable Disease
Description: Catch-all alert category for novel or rare threats.

Name: Respiratory Syncytial Virus
Description: Common respiratory virus; severe in infants/elderly.
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period for RSV ranges from two to eight days, with four to six days being the most common.(2)

Name: Rickettsial Infections
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period for the spotted fever Rickettsia species range from two to 21 days, possibly depending on the size of the inoculum.(3)

Name: Rotavirus
Description: Viral gastroenteritis in young children; vaccine-preventable.

Name: Rubella
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period is from 14 to 17 days, with a range of 12 to 23 days.(3,5)

Name: Salmonellosis
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period is commonly 12–36 hours with a range of 6–72 hours.(1,2) Longer incubation periods of up to 16 days have been documented and may be more common following low dose ingestion of the organism.(1)

Name: Severe Acute Respiratory Infection (SARI)
Description: Surveillance syndrome for severe respiratory infection.
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: Unknown.

Name: Shellfish Poisoning
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: PSP: Symptoms may occur within a few minutes and up to 10 hours after ingestion.(3) ASP: Symptoms usually occur 30 minutes to six hours after ingestion.(2)

Name: Shigellosis
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: Generally, the incubation period for Shigella is usually 1–3 days but can range from 12–96 hours (4 days).(1,3) The incubation period for S. dysenteriae can be longer, up to one week.

Name: St. Louis Encephalitis
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period of SLE is usually four to 21 days.

Name: Streptococcal Disease Group A, Invasive
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period is not clearly defined and may depend on the route of inoculation.

Name: Subacute Sclerosing Panencephalitis
Description: Rare fatal late complication of measles.
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: Approximately 10.8 years. The onset usually occurs seven to 11 years after wild-type measles infection.(2) Patients with adult onset of SSPE present at a mean age of 24.4 years (range 20–35 years).(7)

Name: Syphilis
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period for primary syphilis is 3–90 days, averaging about three weeks.(8)

Name: Tetanus
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period is typically 3–21 days but it may range from one day to several months. A shorter incubation period is associated with more severe disease, and poor prognosis.(1,8)

Name: Toxoplasmosis, Congenital
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period for a congenitally acquired infection is not known.

Name: Trichinosis
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: Systemic symptoms caused by the larvae appear eight to 15 days after ingestion of infected flesh (meat) with a range of five to 45 days. The timeframe depends on the number of parasites involved.

Name: Tuberculosis
Description: Airborne bacterial infection; reportable to TB On-call.
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: Detection of infection can occur 2 – 10 weeks after exposure. Both the TST and IGRA are used to detect latent TB infection, although the IGRA has a higher specificity than the TST in those who have received BCG vaccination.

Name: Tularemia
Description: Zoonotic bacterial infection; multiple exposure routes.
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period averages three to five days, but ranges from one to 21 days.(4,6)

Name: Typhoid Fever
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period is usually 8–14 days but can range from three to more than 60 days, depending on the size of infecting dose and host factors.(1)

Name: Varicella (Chickenpox)
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period is generally 14–16 days but may range from 10 days to three weeks (21 days).(2) This period may be prolonged for as long as 28 days in persons who have received post-exposure treatment (e.g., varicella-zoster immune gl

Name: Vibrio Cholerae (non-O1, non-O139)
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period is short, ranging from 12 to 24 hours.

Name: Vibrio Parahaemolyticus
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period is generally between 12 and 24 hours but may range between four and 30 hours.

Name: Viral Haemorrhagic Fever
Description: Group of severe viral haemorrhagic fevers.

Name: West Nile Virus
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: Symptoms usually develop within 2 to 6 days but can range from 2 to 14 days and up to several weeks in immunocompromised individuals.(1–3,10)

Name: Western Equine Encephalitis
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period of WEE is usually five to 15 days.

Name: Yellow Fever
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period is typically three to six days. Persons may be viremic before they demonstrate symptoms.

Name: Yersiniosis
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period is most often three to seven days, generally less than 10 days.(4)

Name: Zika Virus
Communicable Disease::Incubation Period: The incubation period ranges from 3 – 12 days.(3)
